data.content_ref: inline:The target's core claim that strict advantage arises iff I(C;B) > I(C;M_wrong) is a non-sequitur for this problem's loss regime. The problem explicitly demands an account of how filtering can LOSE when false flags erode correct majorities. The target's information-theoretic inequality I(C;B) > I(C;M_wrong) provides no mechanism for the loss case: if I(C;B) is high (flags perfectly track individual badness), the inequality holds, but if the critic still produces false positives on correct candidates in a 3/2 correct majority, filtering can still erode the majority and cause a loss. The global inequality ignores the per-question majority margin mechanics required to predict the loss regime, failing the problem's explicit requirement to model the loss condition.
